column2: Establish a national, uniform standard of care to prevent regional disparities in treatment availability
Coverage and access to medically accepted treatments must be uniform across all states and regions
States may not deny or restrict access to nationally approved treatments based on political or ideological differences
If a treatment is unavailable locally, patients must be allowed to access care across state lines without restriction
If coverage is denied or unavailable locally, the system must provide travel and accommodation support to access care elsewhere
National standards must ensure consistent interpretation and application of evidence-based care across all regions
Geography must not determine access to fundamental rights, healthcare, or core public services
A national baseline of rights and services must be uniformly guaranteed across all states and territories
States may not restrict, deny, or degrade access to federally guaranteed rights or services
Individuals must be able to access rights and services across state lines without restriction
Systems must provide logistical support (travel, tele-access, etc.) when local access is unavailable
Federal law must preempt state law where state-level policies create unequal access to protected rights or services
Systems with high risk of centralized abuse must not be fully controlled by a single federal authority
Elections must remain primarily administered at the state and local level to prevent centralized manipulation
Federal government may set standards and enforce safeguards but must not directly control election administration
Election systems must include multiple independent layers of oversight to prevent capture at any single level
Safeguards must exist against both state-level suppression and federal-level manipulation
Election administration authority must be distributed across multiple independent entities to prevent single-point control
